title: How to Navigate Psychedelic Therapy When You Have a History of Self-Harm
body:
How to Move through Psychedelic Therapy When You Have a History of Self-Harm

If you’ve carried the weight of self-harm in your past, stepping into psychedelic therapy can feel like walking into a room full of mirrors. You see yourself, but fractured, raw, maybe even scared of what might come up. Right?! You’re not alone in this. Many folks with histories of self-injury wonder how to approach psychedelic therapy safely and meaningfully. The truth is, psychedelics can be powerful tools for healing, but they demand care, preparation, and honest conversations.

Look, this isn’t about sugarcoating things or telling you psychedelics are a magic wand. They’re not. They can bring up all sorts of feelings - sometimes messy, sometimes beautiful. When you’ve self-harmed before, your relationship with pain, control, and your body is often complicated. Psychedelic therapy doesn’t erase that. Instead, it can shine a spotlight on it, making it essential to go in with eyes wide open.

Why Psychedelic Therapy Can Trigger Old Wounds

Imagine a moment in therapy when a simple question stirs up a flood of memories you thought were buried. Psychedelic therapy can do that on steroids. It loosens the usual mental filters, making you face parts of yourself you might usually avoid. For someone with a history of self-harm, this can mean confronting old urges, traumas, or feelings of shame.

Here’s the thing, your brain keeps a record of trauma in your body and nervous system. This is why The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) by Bessel van der Kolk is a must-read before you dive in. It explains how trauma and pain aren’t just memories but physical experiences stored in your cells. Psychedelic therapy can help release some of that, but it can also stir it up.

That stirring up might look like sudden emotional flooding or intense physical sensations - things that could trigger self-harm urges. So, the question is: how do you prepare for this? How do you keep yourself safe?

Start With an Honest Assessment

Before you even think about scheduling a session, get real with yourself and your therapist. This means sharing your history of self-harm - not just the fact it happened, but how you’ve managed since then. Did you self-injure recently? How often? What triggers you? What coping mechanisms do you have now?

A good psychedelic therapist will ask these questions. They need to know your full story to help you stay safe. If you’re not working with a therapist yet, find one experienced in trauma, and ideally in psychedelic-assisted therapy too. You can start by checking resources from MAPS, which has directories of trained professionals.

Don’t hold back. This is your health and your safety on the line. I’ve seen people shy away from telling their therapist about self-harm because of shame or fear of judgment. That’s a huge mistake. You want someone who can guide you through the tough parts, not someone who’ll be caught off guard mid-session.

Prepare Your Support System

Look, psychedelic therapy isn’t a solo journey. You need support before, during, and after your sessions. This might be a friend, a family member, or a therapist who understands your history and your goals. Having someone you trust to check in with can be lifesaving if things get intense.

Some people even create a “safety plan” with their support person. This plan spells out what to do if you feel overwhelmed or if urges to self-harm come up. It could include calling a therapist, using grounding exercises, or even just having a distraction ready - like a playlist or a favorite book. I often recommend A Really Good Day by Ayelet Waldman (paid link) to people looking for relatable stories about mental health and mood changes. It’s honest and practical, which is exactly what you need.

Also, consider your physical environment. Is it a place where you feel safe? Comfortable? If you’re doing microdosing or lower-dose sessions on your own, make sure your space is clear of anything you might use to hurt yourself. This might feel extreme, but it’s part of creating a container where you can let go a little without risk.

Practice Grounding and Self-Soothing Techniques

Before your session, build up a toolbox of grounding and self-soothing strategies. When emotions run high, these tools become your lifeline. Things like deep breathing, mindfulness, or even physical tools like an acupressure mat and pillow set (paid link) can help reduce anxiety and bring you back to your body in a gentle way.

It’s also a good idea to practice these techniques regularly before your session. That way, when you’re in the thick of it, you don’t have to remember new skills - you already know them. People with histories of self-harm sometimes have to retrain their brain on how to handle distress. Psychedelic therapy can help with that retraining, but you need a foundation to stand on.

Set Clear Intentions for Your Therapy

Intentions aren’t just spiritual fluff. They guide your experience and give you something solid to come back to when things get confusing. Write down what you want from your sessions. Maybe it’s understanding your self-harm urges better. Maybe it’s healing old wounds or learning new coping skills. Whatever it is, be specific.

Here’s an example: “I want to explore the emotions behind my self-harm urges without judgment and learn how to care for my body instead of hurting it.” That’s clear. That’s actionable. Does that land?

Keep your intentions visible during your session if possible. A note on your wall, a phrase in your journal, or a reminder on your phone can bring you back if your mind wanders into dark places.

Communicate With Your Therapist Before and After Sessions

Open communication is key. Don’t wait until a session to bring up your concerns or questions. Talk with your therapist about what you’re feeling leading up to the experience. If you notice self-harm urges increasing, let them know. If you feel scared or unsure, speak up.

And afterward? Check-ins are crucial. Psychedelic experiences can leave you feeling vulnerable for days. You might uncover feelings you weren’t ready for or relive trauma. Your therapist can help you process these safely. In fact, sessions after the psychedelic experience are often where the real integration happens.

If you want to get deeper into the how and why of integration, I wrote a detailed guide you might find useful: The Art of Psychedelic Integration. It covers concrete steps to make your experience meaningful without being overwhelmed.

Prepare for Possible Challenges

Honestly, no journey is without bumps. You might experience flashbacks, increased anxiety, or temporary spikes in self-harm urges. That’s tough. But it doesn’t mean you failed or that psychedelic therapy isn’t for you.

One client I worked with, let’s call her Maya, had a history of cutting that she hadn’t done in years. After a psychedelic session, she felt a sudden urge she hadn’t expected. Instead of hiding it, she called her therapist immediately. They talked it through, and Maya used grounding techniques to ride the wave. It didn’t disappear overnight, but the urge eventually passed. That moment of reaching out made all the difference.

Be ready for those moments. Have a plan. Know who you’ll call. Prepare your grounding tools. And remember: urges are just urges. They don’t have to become actions.

Consider Microdosing First

If jumping into full-dose psychedelic therapy feels too intense, microdosing might be a safer place to start. It involves taking very low doses of psychedelics that don’t cause hallucinations but can shift your mood and awareness subtly.

Microdosing lets you explore your reactions in a controlled way. You can notice how your body and mind respond without overwhelming yourself. Many people with trauma histories find this approach more manageable. You can also keep your therapist in the loop during microdosing to monitor progress and challenges.

Just be sure to research and source your substances carefully. The field is still unregulated, and quality matters. For more on microdosing safely, check out the detailed resources on Scientific American.

Know When Psychedelic Therapy Might Not Be Right

Look, psychedelic therapy isn’t for everyone. If you’re currently in crisis, actively self-harming, or have severe mental health conditions like psychosis, it might not be safe to proceed. This isn’t a failure or a sign you’re broken. It’s just reality.

Being honest with yourself and your healthcare providers about where you’re at emotionally is the best way to protect yourself. There’s no shame in waiting, building more stability, or exploring other therapies first.

Wrapping Up: Your Safety Comes First

Psychedelic therapy can offer deep insights and healing if approached thoughtfully. If you have a history of self-harm, it takes courage to even consider it. Know what I mean?

Here’s the short list for moving forward safely:

  • Be transparent with your therapist about your history and current state.
  • Build a trustworthy support network and a clear safety plan.
  • Practice grounding and self-soothing techniques before your session.
  • Set clear, specific intentions for your therapy.
  • Communicate openly before and after sessions.
  • Consider starting with microdosing if full doses feel overwhelming.
  • Know when it’s okay to wait or choose different treatments.

Remember, this is your healing journey. You get to decide the pace and the tools you use. And if you want to learn more about consciousness and healing, swing by my site. There’s a lot of heart and honest advice waiting for you.

For a deeper dive into consciousness and mindfulness practice that complements psychedelic therapy, check out Waking Up by Sam Harris (paid link). It’s a solid guide to understanding your mind beyond the psychedelic experience.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
